title: VERRA
shortDescription: A fictional urban streetwear identity for VERRA, built around a single wordmark where one bird-shaped mark forms both the V and, flipped, the A.
challenge: VERRA needed a mark aggressive enough for streetwear, but disciplined enough to survive being printed onto everything from a woven tag to a tote bag.
approach: The wordmark was built entirely by hand rather than adapted from an existing typeface: the V redrawn as a stylised bird in flight, then flipped to double as the A, so it reads as one continuous shape rather than five separate letters, balanced, but still sharp and urban.
result: One symbol carried across the full range: labels, tags, stickers, apparel and accessories, each piece still reading unmistakably as VERRA at a glance.

title: PANDA PIXELS
shortDescription: A full brand identity for a fictional cookie brand aimed at kids with a strong pull toward tech and gaming, built entirely from scratch for a school brief.
challenge: The brief called for a cookie brand that could hold a young, game-obsessed audience's attention: fun enough to grab them, structured enough to actually work as a real brand system.
approach: Built a panda mascot kitted out with VR goggles and a jetpack as the brand's core character, then carried it across packaging and a custom logo. The site itself was designed first in Figma, then translated into a fully custom website, hand-coded from scratch in plain HTML, CSS and JavaScript with its own animations, for both desktop and mobile.
result: A playful, strategically built system carried consistently from packaging to screen, mascot, logo, packaging and site all reading as one brand rather than separate deliverables.

title: GFIETST
shortDescription: A World Bike Day campaign for Gfietst, giving cyclists with physical and mental disabilities real visibility on the road, on screen, and in the press.
challenge: Gfietst needed the campaign to do more than sit on a poster: real recognition for people with disabilities who cycle, timed to Flanders' World Bike Day, across print, digital and the street itself.
approach: Laid the campaign out around one key visual: a standard bicycle traffic light Photoshopped into a lineup of Gfietst's adapted bikes, with a custom icon set and motion versions for digital and screen placements. The idea then moved off the page into a PR stunt: a bike-path stencil sprayed onto the street the same way ordinary bike lane markings are, and street signs taken over with magnetic stickers, both swapping in the adapted bikes.
result: The stunt got picked up by the press and covered by VRT NWS, giving the campaign reach well beyond the print and digital materials alone.

title: VLAANDERENLEEST
shortDescription: An online and offline campaign for VlaanderenLeest, built around the concept 'lezen in vervoering': reading while you're being transported.
challenge: The campaign needed an actual idea, not just execution: a name and a concept strong enough to hold together posters, social posts, flyers and a physical reading kit as one thing.
approach: The concept, 'lezen in vervoering', plays on the Dutch word vervoering, which means both being transported and being swept away. The campaign showed people reading everywhere they're literally being transported, on the bus, carpooling in the car, and beyond, then laid the full poster out in close collaboration with an illustrator for the artwork. The same idea carried through Instagram posts, flyers, a magazine ad, station posters and a reading kit.
result: A cohesive, playful campaign that read the same whether you passed it on a station poster, scrolled past it on Instagram, or held the reading kit in your hands.

title: LUMORA
shortDescription: A light-themed event identity for MSK Gent, anchored in a poster, a website design and a motion social ad, all built around a single glowing key visual.
challenge: LUMORA needed a visual language built entirely around light for an MSK Gent event, modern and sleek, but still carrying real art-historical weight.
approach: Built the key visual around Emile Claus's painting De IJsvogel (The Kingfisher): its color palette and the shape of the river reworked into a cool, gradient-like form that reads as both projection and light. Extended the same key visual into a poster, a desktop and mobile website design, and a motion version for a social media ad.
result: One glowing shape carrying the whole event, poster, website and social motion all reading as the same idea at a glance.

title: CAPYBARISTA
shortDescription: A playful brand identity for Capybarista, a coffee bar where you can cuddle capybaras, cat-cafe style.
challenge: Capybarista needed a brand as playful and huggable as the concept itself: a coffee bar built around cuddling capybaras.
approach: Built the logo around a cup of coffee where the negative space in the milk foam reveals a capybara's head, then carried a reusable S-shaped coffee stain across the rest of the branding as a recurring mark. Extended the system into stationery, an Instagram presence, a storefront and merch like mugs.
result: A cohesive, playful identity where even the smallest touch, a coffee stain shaped like the brand's own initial, ties everything back to the same idea.

title: THUNDER VALLEY
shortDescription: A bold, brutalist website for Thunder Valley, a rock festival built for a younger audience, spanning a homepage and a dedicated app-discovery page across desktop and mobile.
challenge: Thunder Valley needed a site with the same raw energy as the festival itself: a bold, brutalist identity that could still guide a young audience clearly from homepage to lineup to tickets, on both desktop and mobile.
approach: Built the full sitemap and UX flow from scratch, then designed a bold, brutalist layout anchored in oversized type, sharp red accents and high-contrast black to carry the festival's energy. Every screen, from the homepage to a dedicated app-discovery page, was tested on real people and refined based on how they actually used it, across both desktop and mobile.
result: A website and companion app-discovery flow that reads as loud and confident as the festival it represents, validated on real users rather than assumptions, and built to work just as well in the hand as it does on a full-size screen.
